title: Data & Tools
text: This page highlights software, data resources, and reproducible workflows connected to my research and teaching.

title: Data & Tools
text:
R packages
I develop and maintain R tools for demographic analysis and simulation:

Rcompadre: an R interface to the COMPADRE and COMADRE databases, enabling users to access, filter, and work with large collections of matrix population models. GitHub | CRAN
Rage: tools for calculating life-history traits from matrix population models, including survival, reproductive, and life-table analyses. GitHub | CRAN
mpmsim: tools for simulating matrix population models under user-defined demographic scenarios for teaching, sensitivity analysis, and methods development. GitHub | CRAN

title: Data & Tools
text:
Demographic databases
COMPADRE (plants) and COMADRE (animals) are open, curated repositories of matrix population models used in comparative demography worldwide. I have co-led both projects since 2010. Together they contain thousands of population models from hundreds of species and underpin a growing body of demographic research. Access both databases at compadre-db.org.

title: Data & Tools
text:
Reproducible workflows
I prioritise transparent, version-controlled workflows for ecological analysis, including demographic modelling pipelines, phenology workflows, and clear computational documentation.

title: Data & Tools
text:
Open data and archived code
Where possible, data and code are shared through stable repositories to support reuse, scrutiny, and cumulative science. This includes archived analysis code and teaching materials in R.

title: Students & Collaboration
text: I welcome enquiries from prospective students and collaborators whose interests align with population demography, life-history evolution, phenology, and quantitative ecology.

title: Students & Collaboration
text:
Before contacting me
Please read my research overview and send a concise message explaining your specific interests, relevant background, and why there is a good fit with my work.
Include:

your current role and institution
a short description of your research interests or project idea
relevant methods or data experience (for example R, modelling, field/lab systems)
practical details such as expected timing and funding context (if known)

title: Students & Collaboration
text:
Prospective PhD students
Strong enquiries usually include a focused problem, awareness of the relevant literature, and clear motivation for joining this research area. If you are considering doctoral study, outline your interests in one or two well-defined directions rather than a broad generic statement.

title: Students & Collaboration
text:
Collaboration enquiries
I am open to collaborations that are methodologically clear and scientifically specific, especially where there is strong complementarity in data, theory, or analytical expertise.

title: Students & Collaboration
section: A brief note on email style
text:
A brief note on email style
Focused, tailored emails are much more useful than generic mass emails. A short, well-prepared message is the best starting point.

title: Research
text: My research programme centres on demography, life-history evolution, phenology, and quantitative methods, with emphasis on robust inference and open, reusable scientific infrastructure.

title: Research
text:
Demography and life history evolution
I study how survival, development, and reproduction combine to shape population trajectories within and across taxa, drawing on long-term field datasets and comparative resources such as COMPADRE and COMADRE. A central aim is to understand how variation in vital rates drives the evolution of life-history strategies across the tree of life.

title: Research
section: Ageing across the tree of life
text:
Ageing across the tree of life
I work on comparative questions about senescence and longevity: why ageing trajectories differ so markedly across species, and what this reveals about life-history evolution. This includes work published in Nature (2014) documenting substantial diversity in ageing trajectories across the tree of life.

title: Research
text:
Phenology and climate change
A substantial strand of my research examines the timing of life-cycle events and how phenological responses to environmental change affect population dynamics and species interactions.

title: Research
text:
Open data infrastructure
I co-lead the COMPADRE Plant and COMADRE Animal Matrix Database projects and contribute tools that make demographic data easier to access, analyse, and reproduce.

title: Research
text:
Quantitative methods
I contribute to methods that integrate demographic, phylogenetic, and phenological data at scale, with particular attention to reproducible workflows in R.

title: Research
text:
Current project areas
Current funded projects include a DFF-supported study of drivers of individual variation in woodland phenology under climate change, and a Novo Nordisk Foundation sabbatical project using large open datasets to quantify global phenological shifts. Ongoing collaborative work addresses the diversity of ageing across taxa, links between social organisation and lifespan in mammals, and continued development of the COMPADRE and COMADRE databases.

title: Teaching
text:
My teaching interests are centred on quantitative ecology, demographic methods, and clear scientific communication.
Likely teaching areas include:

Population Biology and Evolution
Introduction to Biodemography
Quantitative Conservation Ecology
Data Handling, Visualisation and Statistics

I support open teaching materials where appropriate, particularly for quantitative ecology and R-based analysis.

title: Home
text:
Associate Professor, Department of Biology, University of Southern Denmark
Currently on sabbatical at the University of Edinburgh (2025-2026)


I am an Associate Professor at the University of Southern Denmark, currently on sabbatical at the University of Edinburgh. My research focuses on the diversity of life histories, demographic processes, and phenological patterns across the tree of life, addressing why some species live long lives while others have short ones, and how organisms time life-cycle events in response to environmental change. Demography lies at the core of this work, from single-population analyses (including Bayesian Survival Trajectory Analysis) to macrodemographic comparisons across populations and species. Alongside this, I work on phenology and climate change and help build open research infrastructure, including the COMPADRE and COMADRE projects and the R packages Rcompadre, Rage, and mpmsim.



Google Scholar
ORCID
GitHub
SDU profile

Prizes: BES Elton Prize (2016) and PNAS Cozzarelli Prize (2016)



Current work focuses on demography and life-history evolution, ageing across the tree of life, phenology under climate change, and quantitative methods for integrating demographic, phylogenetic, and phenological data.
Current funding: Danish Independent Research Fund (DFF) project on individual variation in woodland phenology under climate change; Novo Nordisk Foundation sabbatical project on global phenological shifts from large open datasets.



I maintain and contribute to open tools and workflows for demographic analysis, including R-based resources and reproducible research practices. I co-lead the COMPADRE and COMADRE databases, which are used internationally in comparative demographic research (including across more than 60 countries; update this figure if needed). See Data & Tools for an overview.



I welcome focused enquiries from prospective students and collaborators with shared interests in demography, population ecology, and quantitative methods. See Students & Collaboration for guidance.






Owen R. Jones
